text.title: WebSocket Server Connection Manager
text.summary: Server-side connection management: connect/disconnect lifecycle, channel subscriptions, and broadcast patterns.

rule.statement: Authenticate on the WebSocket upgrade handshake before calling `accept()`. Never accept anonymous connections and authorize later.
rule.rationale: Once `accept()` is called, the connection is established and consumes a file descriptor. Validating auth before acceptance allows the HTTP layer to return 401/403 with no WS overhead.

rule.statement: Validate the `Origin` header server-side against an allowlist. Same-origin is not enforced for WebSocket upgrades by browsers.
rule.rationale: WebSocket upgrade requests bypass the browser's same-origin CSRF protections. An attacker can craft a page that initiates a WS connection to your server from any origin.

rule.statement: On disconnect, clean up all channel subscriptions atomically. Stale user IDs in channel subscription maps cause broadcasts to dead connections and memory leaks.
rule.rationale: A subscription map that retains disconnected users wastes memory and causes errors on send attempts. The disconnect handler must iterate all user channels and remove memberships.

rule.statement: Check connection state before sending (`ws.client_state == CONNECTED`). Sending to a closed WebSocket raises an exception and must be caught.
rule.rationale: Disconnect and send can race: the connection drops between the state check and the actual send. Always wrap send in a try/except and disconnect on error.

section.title: Message Envelope

rule.statement: Use a versioned typed message envelope: `{ v, type, channel, data, id }`. The `type` field drives routing; `channel` scopes delivery.
rule.rationale: Untyped messages require downstream parsing to determine handling. A versioned envelope allows protocol upgrades without breaking existing clients — clients ignore unknown `v` values gracefully.

section.reference: FastAPI ConnectionManager with channel subscriptions and broadcast
